content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 3, 2019


Jason Zaragoza
Deputy City Attorney
305 West Third Street, Suite 100 E
Oxnard, CA 93030

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-19-078

Dear Mr. Zaragoza:

       This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Oxnard City Manager Alexander
Nguyen regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

       Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should
contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

       Does the Act prohibit the City Manager from participating in the City's decisions regarding
the future operations and management of the City's municipal facility River Ridge Golf Course
("Golf Course") where the City Manager owns a house ("Property") that is located within 500 feet
of the Golf Course?

CONCLUSION

       While the Act prohibits the City Manager from participating in decisions relating to the Golf
Course budget and contract with the Golf Course operator, he may be able to participate in
decisions relating to the use of specific course facilities, such as the Clubhouse, so long as they are
not inextricably interrelated to decisions that relate to the course as a whole.


_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-078
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City-owned Golf Course comprises two 18-hole golf courses, the Vineyard Course
("Vineyard") and the Victoria Lakes Course ("Victoria Lakes"). The original 18-hole course,
Vineyard, was developed in 1986. The newer 18-hole course, Victoria Lakes, was constructed
over two phases with the original nine holes opening in 2004. The second nine holes opened in
2008. The Golf Course includes an all-grass driving range, two practice putting greens and two
chipping greens. Additionally, the Golf Course has a clubhouse on Vineyard Avenue, comprising a
pro shop, snack bars, a restaurant, meeting rooms, and a banquet area (the "Facilities"). Although
the City owns the Golf Course, a private company manages, maintains and operates the Golf Course
and the Facilities. The current operator has been in place since 1994. The operator's agreement ends
in June 2019, at which point the City will secure a new Operator.

The City manager owns a home located 291 feet from the Victoria Lakes' property line, and
475 feet from the nearest hole on the course, the 8th hole. The Property is located 4,574 feet from
the Clubhouse.

If permissible under the Act, the City Manager would play a key role in bringing Oxnard's
Golf Course out of deficit: he would participate in procuring a new Golf Course operator,
negotiating the operator's contract on behalf of the City, providing input on the Operator's Golf
Course business plan, providing input on specific operator services, assigning a project manager to
administer and monitor the operator's contract, opining on uses of the Golf Course facilities,
providing input on the operator's marketing plan, and overseeing other aspects of the Golf Course
operations as deemed necessary. Further, every fiscal year, the City Manager proposes a budget to
be adopted by the City Council, which includes a funding recommendation for ongoing Golf Course
operations; the City Manager would continue to serve in that capacity.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public
official's interests set forth in Section 87103. Specific to the provided facts, the following interests
are relevant:

• An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. (Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal
finances.²

2 While an official also has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate family
member (Section 87103), an effect on an official's personal finances is not considered separately where the decision
affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Therefore, we do not analyze
the City Manager's interest in his personal finances.

File No. A-19-078
Page No. 3

Based on the provided facts, the City Manager has a real property interest in his house, assuming he has an interest of $2,000 or more.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Foreseeability standards vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a decision before the official or the official's agency.³ (Regulation 18701(a).) For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) The City Manager's interests are not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue.

Regulation 18702.2 contains the materiality standards for the foreseeable effect of a decision on an official's real property interest. Specific to the provided facts, Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) provides that a financial effect is material where the governmental decision involves property located within 500 feet of the line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's parcel.

Based on the provided facts, the City Manager's home is located within 500 feet of the Golf Course. Your facts indicate that the Golf Course is currently operating in deficit. Any budget decisions involving the Golf Course in which the City Manager is involved would presumably help to stabilize operational expresses and keep the Golf Couse financially viable and operational. Ruling the case, the Act prohibits the City Manager from taking part in decisions relating to the Golf Course budget because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his residential real property interest, because he owns real property located with 500 feet of the course. (See the de Bortnowsky Advice Letter, No. A-18-054).

Implementation Decisions

You have also asked whether the City Manager would be able to participate in decisions related to procuring a new Golf Course operator, negotiating the operator's contract on behalf of the City, providing input on the Operator's Golf Course business plan, providing input on specific operator services, assigning a project manager to administer and monitoring the operator's contract, and providing input on the operator's marketing plan.

The foreseeability and materiality analysis will generally be applied on a decision-by-decision basis. We have previously advised that under certain circumstances, decisions implementing projects may be treated separately from the decision on the project itself. Implementation decisions merely implement, or carry out, decisions already made. (Boga Advice Letter, No. A-03-067.) We have further advised that where an individual is disqualified from basic

³ Regulation 18701(a) establishes that a financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

File No. A-19-078
Page No. 4

policy decisions, he or she may participate in implementation decisions so long as all of the
following apply:

(1) The decisions for which he has a disqualifying financial interest are
segregated and decided first;
(2) The remaining decisions will not result in reopening or in any way
affect the decisions from which he was disqualified; and
(3) The decisions will not independently have a material financial effect on
his economic interests.

(Warne Advice Letter, No. I-02-052; Olson Advice Letter, No. A-00-237.)

The specific decisions at issue here are not the decision concerning the Golf Course budget,
but instead, decisions to select the Golf Course operator, negotiating the operator's contract on
behalf of the City, providing input on the Operator's Golf Course business plan, providing input on
specific operator services, assigning a project manager to administer and monitoring the operator's
contract, and providing input on the operator's marketing plan. Based upon the facts provided, the
Golf Course is currently operating at a deficiency and the selection of the operator including an
assessment of the operator's proposed business and marketing plan appear very pertinent to the
future viability of the course. Accordingly, at least prior to the selection of the operator, it appears
that these decisions may have an independent material effect on the City Manager's residence and
the City Manager would not be able to take part in the decisions.

However, we note that the City Manager may be able to participate in other decisions and
reviews of the operator to the extent that the Golf Course budget has already been fixed and the
selection of the operator has already been made. However, any decision will have to be reviewed on
a case by case basis to determine if it the decision may have a material effect on the City Manager's
residence. If the City Manager needs assistance determining whether he may participate in future
decisions involving the review of the operator's performance, he may wish to seek additional
advice describing the specific decision.

Potential Segmentation:

Under certain circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate
in other related decisions if the official's participation does not affect the decision in which he or
she has a conflict of interest. (Regulation 18706.) The Commission has consistently advised that an
official may segment a decision in which the official has a conflict of interest from other decisions
in which he or she does not have a conflict of interest to allow participation by the official in one or
several related decisions if the decisions are not too interrelated to be considered separately.

The Commission has advised that some decisions may be too interrelated and may not be
considered separately, such as when resolution of one decision will effectively determine, affirm,
nullify or alter the result of the other decision. (Regulation 18706(b).) Segmentation may only
apply if the decisions can be broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably
interrelated to the decisions in which you have a disqualifying conflict of interest.

File No. A-19-078
Page No. 5

As discussed in the Henderson Advice Letter, No. I-13-093, which also analyses a potential conflict involving capital improvements at a City golf course, an exception exists to the literal boundary-to-boundary measurement under the 500-foot rule, applicable in cases where the governmental decision affects only a clearly defined, specific, and isolated site, such as a particular building on a large tract of land.¹ You have also noted that the City Manager's tasks will include opining on uses of the Golf Course facilities including the use of the Clubhouse, located 4,575 feet away from is residence. The Act would therefore not prohibit the City Manager from taking part in management decisions relating to the use of specific course facilities, such as the clubhouse, so long as they are not inextricably interrelated to decisions that relate to the course as a whole.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[Signature]

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja

¹ The Henderson Advice letter was incorrect on generally concluding that a decision on the golf course budget is not a decision with a potential effect on real property and is therefore superseded in this regard. In this case, the budget decision and selection of a course operator may ultimately keep the Golf Course financially viable and operational. Thus, these decisions have a potential effect on real property, which must be considered.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public 
official's interests set forth in Section 87103. Specific to the provided facts, the following interests 
are relevant: 
• 
An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. (Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal 
finances. 2 
2 While an ~fficial also has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate family 
member (Section 87103), an effect on an official's personal finances is not considered separately where the decision 
affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation 18702.S(c).) Therefore, we do not analyze 
the City Manager's interest in his personal finances.  
Based on the provided facts, the City Manager has a real property interest in his house, 
assuming he has an interest of $2,000 or more. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Foreseeability standards vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a 
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
decision before the official or the official's agency.3 (Regulation 18701(a).) For a financial interest 
that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an 
official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) The City Manager's interests are not

Regulation 18702.2 contains the materiality standards for the foreseeable effect of a decision 
on an official's real property interest. Specific to the provided facts, Regulation 18702.2(a)(7)

Based on the provided facts, the City Manager's home is located within 500 feet of the Golf

decisions involving the Golf Couse in which the City Manager is involved would presumably help
